fabricated_divergences.quote: English-language sources from Qatar (Al Jazeera), Germany (DW News), and the UK (The Guardian) include the Taliban spokesman's condemnation ('cowardly act of aggression') alongside Pakistan's claims, while English-language sources from France (France 24) and Japan (Japan Today) and the Italian-language source (ANSA, article 0) present primarily or exclusively the Pakistani government's framing without substantive Taliban rebuttal.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: France 24 (article 5) explicitly notes 'Kabul's accusation that Pakistani airstrikes have caused civilian casualties,' which is a substantive Taliban/Afghan rebuttal, so placing it in the 'no substantive Taliban rebuttal' group is unsupported. Also, Al Jazeera (article 1) does not carry the 'cowardly act of aggression' condemnation; its Afghan angle is the Taliban's 'denial of harboring militants,' not the condemnation attributed here.

fabricated_divergences.quote: The Italian outlet (ANSA) splits the story into two separate single-source articles — one carrying only Pakistan's claim of 25 killed militants and another carrying only the Taliban's civilian-casualty condemnation — while English-language outlets from Qatar, Germany, the UK and Japan integrate both the Pakistani and Taliban narratives within single articles.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Japan Today (article 8) does not integrate any Taliban narrative: its only quoted actors are Pakistan's information minister and a Jamaat-ul-Ahrar claim of responsibility, with no Taliban government perspective or civilian-casualty condemnation. So the claim that the Japanese outlet integrates both narratives is not supported by the analysis.
